### Related literature {#sec-literature}

This paper sits at the intersection of several well-developed literatures in finance, economics, and industrial organization. No existing model combines all six features---real options under uncertainty, endogenous default risk, oligopoly competition, regime-switching demand, diminishing returns, and two-sided resource allocation---needed to capture the AI compute investment problem. I review each strand in turn, emphasizing the building blocks the present model draws upon and the gaps it fills.

**Irreversible investment and real options.**
The theoretical foundation is the real options literature pioneered in the 1980s. @mcdonald1986value establish the canonical model of optimal timing for an irreversible project whose value follows geometric Brownian motion, showing that firms should wait until project benefits are roughly twice the investment cost before committing. @brennan1985evaluating develop contingent-claims valuation of real assets, treating operating flexibility---the ability to open, close, or mothball---as a portfolio of options. @pindyck1988irreversible extends the analysis to *incremental* capacity choice, showing that each marginal unit of capacity is valued as a separate option and that under moderate uncertainty optimal capacity is substantially smaller than under reversibility---directly relevant to phased GPU cluster additions. @bernanke1983irreversibility introduces the "bad news principle": under irreversibility, the investment decision is driven primarily by worst-case outcomes, implying that AI infrastructure firms should focus on the probability that enterprise AI adoption stalls rather than on upside scenarios. @majd1987time model sequential investment where projects require time to complete, with each expenditure buying an option on the next stage---applicable to data centers requiring two to four years from planning to operation. @barilan1996investment show that with sufficiently long construction lags, the standard relationship between uncertainty and investment can *reverse*: more uncertainty leads to *more* investment because firms must commit early or risk missing demand entirely. The authoritative survey by @pindyck1991irreversibility and the textbook treatment of @dixit1994investment provide the unifying framework; the present paper builds most directly on the capacity-choice structure of @pindyck1988irreversible, extended to a strategic setting.

**Strategic option exercise and oligopoly investment.**
With three to four hyperscalers making simultaneous irreversible bets on GPU capacity, the game-theoretic real options literature provides the sharpest available tools. @grenadier2002option provides the seminal analysis of equilibrium option exercise under Cournot-Nash competition, establishing a "myopic optimality" result and showing that competition *drastically erodes* the option value of waiting---firms invest at near-zero NPV thresholds instead of the large positive thresholds predicted by single-firm models. @grenadier1996strategic shows that rational strategic option exercise produces "development cascades"---concentrated bursts of investment that can occur even during declining demand, endogenously generating overbuilding. @huisman2015strategic, the closest single paper to the AI compute problem among the strategic models, study a duopoly in which firms choose *both* investment timing and capacity level under demand uncertainty, showing that under high uncertainty the first mover always becomes the dominant firm. The present paper extends their framework by incorporating endogenous default risk and diminishing returns.

@bouis2009multistage extend real options games to $N \geq 3$ firms, discovering an "accordion effect" in which demand shocks change the wedge between successive investors' thresholds non-uniformly---a feature I exploit in the $N$-firm equilibrium of @sec-extensions. @aguerrevere2003equilibrium studies incremental capacity investment with time-to-build and operating flexibility in oligopoly, finding that increased uncertainty may *encourage* capacity expansion because operating flexibility makes extra capacity more valuable as a call option on future demand. @kulatilaka1998strategic show that when strategic commitment is valuable, increased uncertainty can encourage investment in growth options. @back2009open provide an important methodological correction: under closed-loop strategies where firms observe rivals' actions, preemption incentives are even stronger than in @grenadier2002option's open-loop framework. @weeds2002strategic analyzes strategic delay in R\&D competition, showing that the interaction of option value and preemption can produce either early investment or excessive delay depending on relative first- and second-mover advantages. See also @fudenberg1985preemption for the game-theoretic foundations and @smit2004strategic for a comprehensive synthesis.

**Default risk, capital structure, and financial constraints.**
A critical feature missing from most strategic investment models is the possibility that aggressive capacity investment financed with debt leads to bankruptcy. The structural credit risk literature originating with @merton1974pricing, @blackcox1976valuing, and @leland1994corporate provides the foundation, with @goldstein2001ebit developing the dynamic framework that permits endogenous default boundaries. @kumar2018optimal jointly optimize financial leverage and irreversible capacity investment, showing that financial leverage and capacity are *substitutes*: firms building more capacity should use less debt, and higher uncertainty reduces both optimal capacity and leverage simultaneously. @hackbarth2014capital combine real options, endogenous default, product market competition, and demand uncertainty---the highest feature-count paper in this cluster, combining four of the six features the present model targets. @sundaresan2015dynamic develop a model with sequential growth options and endogenous default, showing that when existing debt is sufficiently high, firms *risk-shift by prematurely exercising growth options*---overinvesting because equity captures the upside while debt bears the downside. @bolton2011unified and @bolton2019investment model financially constrained firms, showing that investment thresholds depend on cash holdings and that financial constraints amplify the impact of uncertainty on investment.

@hennessy2004tobin demonstrates that debt overhang is more severe for *long-lived assets*---a crucial insight given data centers' 15--20 year lifespans. @mauer1994interactions and @childs2005interactions model how debt financing creates investment distortions through both risk-shifting and debt overhang channels. @miao2005optimal develops an industry equilibrium model combining default risk with competitive dynamics, producing predictions about leverage ratios and default rates. @arnold2013growth show that firms whose value consists primarily of growth options face higher credit risk during downturns. The present model builds on @hackbarth2012corporate for the joint investment-financing framework, integrating endogenous default into the strategic capacity-choice game of @huisman2015strategic.

**Regime switching, adoption timing, and technology diffusion.**
The distinctive feature of AI demand uncertainty is not merely volatility but the *uncertain timing of adoption inflections*. @guo2005investment allow the demand growth rate and volatility to shift between regimes at random Markov-switching times, showing that investment features occasional large jumps when a favorable regime arrives and that different thresholds apply in different regimes---which I use to model the binary nature of AI demand. @bloom2009impact develops a structural model with time-varying volatility shocks, showing that uncertainty shocks cause firms to temporarily "pause" investment. @bloom2007uncertainty provide empirical validation of real options theory using firm-level panel data, confirming that firms facing higher uncertainty respond more weakly to demand shocks. @rob2003foreign model entry with uncertain demand growth where firms choose between flexible high-marginal-cost production and irreversible low-marginal-cost capacity, a structure that maps to the choice between renting cloud GPU instances and building dedicated data centers. @jovanovic2005general document that general-purpose technology adoption features S-curve diffusion taking decades, raising the possibility that current AI capacity buildout may be premature relative to adoption timelines.

**Diminishing returns, scaling laws, and R\&D investment.**
The economics of AI training exhibits clear power-law relationships between compute investment and model capability [@kaplan2020scaling; @hoffmann2022training]. The growth theory literature has studied analogous structures for decades. @bloom2020ideas document across multiple domains that research productivity is declining sharply while research effort rises, finding that sustaining Moore's Law required 18 times more researchers between 1970 and 2020---the closest economic analog to neural scaling laws. @jones1995rdbased generalizes endogenous growth to allow diminishing returns in idea production, with the core insight that doubling R\&D effort does not double output growth mapping directly to the sub-linear relationship between compute and capability. @aghion1992model develop the Schumpeterian quality-ladder model where innovations arrive stochastically via creative destruction---capturing how each new frontier AI model partially obsoletes its predecessors. @kortum1997research models research as random search where the probability of beating the frontier falls as the frontier advances, providing a theoretical foundation for diminishing returns. These insights motivate the concave production function in the present model.

**Two-sided resource allocation: training versus inference.**
The AI compute allocation problem---how to split GPU capacity between training (R\&D) and inference (production)---has direct analogs in the growth theory literature. @akcigit2018growth study multi-product firms simultaneously investing in exploitation and exploration R\&D, showing that exploitation scales more strongly with firm size---mapping to the distinction between inference optimization and training next-generation models. @klette2004innovating model firms as collections of production units subject to creative destruction, with R\&D adding new capabilities while existing lines fund operations. @dellaseta2012optimal combine real options with learning curves, generating scale incentives for early investment even under uncertainty. @grossman1991quality model endogenous allocation between R\&D and manufacturing with quality-ladder dynamics. The growth option decomposition of @berk1999optimal and @carlson2004corporate, in which firm value consists of assets-in-place and growth options with the mix determining systematic risk, provides the asset pricing implications.

**Contribution relative to the literature.**
The present paper makes three advances relative to the closest existing work. First, it integrates *endogenous default risk with strategic capacity choice* in a real options framework---a combination absent from the literature. Papers either model default without strategic capacity competition [@kumar2018optimal; @sundaresan2015dynamic] or competition without default [@grenadier2002option; @huisman2015strategic]. Second, it embeds *two-sided resource allocation* (training versus inference) in a real options game with financial constraints, connecting the growth theory literature [@akcigit2018growth; @klette2004innovating] with the corporate finance literature on investment under uncertainty. Third, the *revealed beliefs* methodology inverts the structural model to extract private information from observable investment decisions---extending revealed preference theory [@samuelson1938note] to a dynamic setting with strategic interaction and default risk.
